tier_2_handbook.definition: Merkel cell carcinoma (MCC) is a rare, aggressive cutaneous neuroendocrine neoplasia. It arises from Merkel cells, which are touch-sensitive neuroendocrine cells in the skin. MCC is characterized by small round cells with sparse cytoplasm, abundant mitoses, and dense core granules on histology [61,93,95,124,155]. Clinically, MCC presents as a rapidly growing, often asymptomatic, red or purple nodule on sun-exposed skin. In a study of 195 patients, 63% of primary lesions had grown rapidly in the three months prior to diagnosis, and correct clinical diagnosis was rare (1%) [7]. MCC can metastasize early, with 63% of primary lesions having grown rapidly in the 3 months prior to diagnosis [7]. Large meta-analyses show that at least half of patients develop lymph node metastases and nearly one third develop distant metastases [8-15]. MCC has a high mortality rate, exceeding melanoma, with 5-year relative or MCC-specific survival rates ranging from 41% to 77% depending on stage at presentation [2,10,13,18-23]. The definitive diagnosis relies on immunohistochemistry (IHC): cytokeratin 20 (CK20) positivity (membranous and/or paranuclear dot-like) and thyroid transcription factor 1 (TTF-1) negativity are usually sufficient [5,6,93,160,162-165]. Additional neuroendocrine markers (e.g., synaptophysin, neurofilament, INSM1, chromogranin) may be used [29,75,80,93,95,160,161,166-173]. MCC is distinct from metastatic small cell lung carcinoma (SCLC) by IHC. The disease can be categorized by clinical presentation: local disease (clinical N0), regional disease (clinical N+, including in-transit disease), and distant metastatic disease (M1). The AJCC 8th edition staging system is used, with T stage based on tumor size and invasion, N stage based on nodal and in-transit metastases, and M stage based on distant metastases [12,66]. The NCCN guidelines emphasize multidisciplinary consultation at a center with specialized expertise for optimal management [MCC-1].

subtypes.name: MCPyV-Positive Merkel Cell Carcinoma
subtypes.description: MCC tumors that harbor Merkel cell polyomavirus (MCPyV) DNA, detected in 43% to 100% of cases depending on the cohort [51-56]. These tumors typically have a lower mutational burden and are less associated with UV damage signatures. They are more common in immunocompetent individuals and may have a better prognosis compared to virus-negative tumors in some studies [57-60]. The viral oncoproteins (large T antigen) are expressed and can be targeted by immunotherapy.
subtypes.key_associations: MCPyV infection; lower UV signature; better prognosis in some studies; association with immunosuppression

subtypes.name: MCPyV-Negative Merkel Cell Carcinoma
subtypes.description: MCC tumors that are negative for MCPyV. They have a much higher mutational burden, enriched for cytosine to thymine (C to T) mutations indicative of UV damage [63-65]. They tend to occur more often on the head, neck, or trunk. MCPyV-negative status has been associated with increased recurrence [55] and decreased MCC-specific survival and overall survival in some studies [57-60], although not all studies confirm this [61,62]. These tumors may harbor mutations in TP53, RB1, and other genes.
subtypes.frequency: Up to 57% of MCC tumors (depending on cohort)
subtypes.key_associations: High UV mutational signature; higher tumor mutation burden; worse prognosis in some studies; head/neck or trunk location

subtypes.name: Combined Merkel Cell Carcinoma
subtypes.description: MCC tumors that are admixed or adjacent to other cutaneous malignancies, most commonly squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), but also basal cell carcinoma, melanoma, actinic keratosis, and Bowen disease [29-32,61,85,92,105-113]. The combined phenotype may have distinct clinical behavior; some studies suggest a higher likelihood of metastasis, disease progression, and death from disease [32].
subtypes.frequency: Not precisely defined; likely a minority of cases
subtypes.key_associations: Concurrent SCC or other skin cancers; possibly more aggressive course; prior non-melanoma skin cancers and immunosuppression

subtypes.name: Unknown Primary Merkel Cell Carcinoma
subtypes.description: MCC presenting with nodal or distant metastases without an identifiable primary cutaneous lesion. These patients have a better outcome compared to those with synchronous known primary in some studies [12,41,175-178]. The NCCN recommendations for pathologic evaluation and management of the nodal basin are the same as for known primary disease [MCC-4].
subtypes.key_associations: Better prognosis; higher tumor mutation burden; elevated markers of immunity [178]

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: Merkel cell carcinoma (MCC) is driven by two distinct molecular pathways: one associated with Merkel cell polyomavirus (MCPyV) and another associated with UV-induced mutagenesis. MCPyV is a double-stranded DNA virus that is clonally integrated into the tumor genome in a significant proportion of MCCs, detected in 43% to 100% of cases depending on the cohort [51-56]. The virus expresses large T antigen (LT) and small T antigen (sT) oncoproteins that inactivate tumor suppressors, including retinoblastoma protein (RB1), and promote cell cycle progression [51]. In MCPyV-positive MCC, the viral oncoproteins are essential for tumor maintenance, and the tumors typically have a low mutational burden. In contrast, MCPyV-negative MCC arises from chronic UV exposure, accumulating a high burden of somatic mutations with a characteristic UV signature (C to T transitions) [63-65]. These tumors frequently harbor mutations in TP53, RB1, and other genes such as PIK3CA, PTEN, and those involved in chromatin remodeling [63-65]. Genomic analyses have shown that MCPyV-negative MCC have a significantly higher tumor mutation burden (TMB) compared to MCPyV-positive tumors [23]. Additionally, copy number alterations and chromosomal abnormalities are more common in MCPyV-negative tumors. Other molecular alterations include overexpression of survivin and mutations in PDGFRα [53]. The expression of programmed death-ligand 1 (PD-L1) is frequently observed in MCC and may contribute to immune evasion. Immunohistochemical markers such as CK20, TTF-1, and neuroendocrine markers (synaptophysin, INSM1, chromogranin) are used for diagnosis [MCC-A]. The molecular classification has therapeutic implications: immunotherapy with PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors is effective across both subtypes, but MCPyV-negative tumors may have a higher likelihood of response due to higher TMB and neoantigen burden. The NCCN guidelines note that in the Stage IV setting, next-generation sequencing (NGS) of the tumor can sometimes reveal relevant and actionable therapeutic targets [MCC-D 4 of 5], [23].

epidemiology.incidence: Merkel cell carcinoma (MCC) is a rare cancer, with approximately 2,488 individuals affected per year in the United States [1]. Despite its rarity, MCC is one of the most aggressive skin cancers, and its incidence is dramatically increasing [1-4]. Changes in environmental risk factors, an aging population, and improved recognition and diagnosis have contributed to the rising incidence [4]. The incidence is higher in geographic areas with higher UV indices [26-32].
epidemiology.mortality: MCC has a high mortality rate, exceeding that of melanoma. The 5-year relative or MCC-specific survival rates range from 41% to 77%, depending on stage at presentation [2,10,13,18-23]. Survival is significantly worse for patients with immunosuppression [19,38,40-47].
epidemiology.trends: Incidence has been increasing over recent decades, both in the United States and internationally [1-4]. The increase is attributed to an aging population, increased UV exposure, and better detection. Mortality rates have also increased, but improvements in therapy (e.g., immunotherapy) may impact future trends.
epidemiology.demographics: MCC is more common in white individuals compared to people from other ethnicities [4,7,8,13,19]. Incidence increases with age, with the majority of cases occurring in older adults (median age ~70 years) [7]. There is a slight male predominance. Immunosuppressed individuals, such as those with solid organ transplants, chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), or HIV infection, are at disproportionately higher risk [33-39]. Approximately 20% of patients under 50 years of age may have germline mutations in cancer-associated genes [116].

risk_factors.detail: Strong evidence implicates sun exposure as a major risk factor, based on increased incidence in geographic areas with higher UV indices, tendency to occur on sun-exposed skin, and frequency of MCC being admixed or adjacent to UV-induced skin lesions [26-32].
risk_factors.strength: Well-established; supported by ecological, clinical, and molecular data (UV mutational signature in MCPyV-negative tumors) [63-65].

risk_factors.detail: MCC is disproportionally more common in immunosuppressed individuals, including solid organ transplant recipients, patients with chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), HIV/AIDS, and those on chronic immunosuppressive therapy [7,33-39]. Immunosuppression is also associated with worse MCC-specific survival [19,38,40-47].
risk_factors.strength: Strong; consistent across multiple studies, with relative risk increases of 5- to 50-fold depending on condition.

risk_factors.detail: MCC incidence increases with age, with the median age at diagnosis around 70 years [4,7,8,13,19]. The aging population contributes to rising incidence.
risk_factors.strength: Established; continuous risk increase with advancing age.

risk_factors.detail: MCC is more likely to occur in white individuals compared to other ethnicities [4,7,8,13,19].
risk_factors.strength: Established; incidence in whites is 10-20 times higher than in darker-skinned populations.

risk_factors.detail: MCPyV is detected in 43% to 100% of MCC tumors and is thought to be etiologic in virus-positive cases [51-56]. However, virus-negative tumors have a distinct molecular pathogenesis driven by UV. MCPyV seropositivity is common in the general population, so infection alone is not sufficient.
risk_factors.strength: Causal role established for virus-positive MCC; but not all MCC are virus-driven.

risk_factors.detail: In patients under 50 years of age with MCC, germline mutations in known cancer-associated genes have been reported in approximately 20% [116]. Genetic counseling should be considered for these patients [MCC-1].
risk_factors.strength: Emerging evidence; specific genes not fully characterized but suggests hereditary predisposition.

risk_factors.detail: MCC incidence is slightly higher in males than females [1,4,8]. Some studies suggest worse outcomes in males, but not consistently.

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Merkel cell carcinoma (MCC) is a rare, aggressive cutaneous neuroendocrine neoplasm that typically presents as a rapidly growing, asymptomatic nodule on sun-exposed skin of the head/neck or extremities [7]. In a landmark study of 195 patients, 88% of MCC tumors were asymptomatic and correct clinical diagnosis was made in only 1% of cases; 56% were initially presumed to be benign cysts or lesions [7]. The lesion is often described as firm, dome-shaped, red-to-purple, with a smooth, shiny surface, sometimes with overlying telangiectasias [19,22]. Rapid growth is a hallmark: 63% of primary lesions had grown rapidly in the 3 months prior to diagnosis [7]. MCC carries a high mortality rate, with 5-year relative or MCC-specific survival ranging from 41% to 77% depending on stage at presentation [2,10,13,18-23]. At least half of patients develop lymph node metastases, and nearly one third develop distant metastases [8-15]. MCC incidence is increasing, with approximately 2488 new cases per year in the United States [1].

symptoms.name: Asymptomatic nodule
symptoms.detail: Most MCC tumors are asymptomatic; the patient or clinician notices a new or changing growth with no associated pain, tenderness, or pruritus in the majority of cases [7].
symptoms.alarm: Low alarm due to lack of symptoms, but rapid growth should prompt evaluation

symptoms.name: Rapid growth
symptoms.detail: Primary lesion often grows rapidly over weeks to months. Patients may report a lesion that has noticeably enlarged in the prior 3 months [7].
symptoms.frequency: 63% of primary lesions had grown rapidly in the 3 months prior to diagnosis [7]
symptoms.alarm: High – rapid growth is a key feature distinguishing MCC from benign lesions

symptoms.name: Occasional pruritus, bleeding, or ulceration
symptoms.detail: Less common symptoms include itching, bleeding, or ulceration of the lesion, especially with larger tumors [7,19].
symptoms.frequency: Not quantified; less common than asymptomatic presentation

signs.name: Firm, dome-shaped, red-to-purple nodule
signs.detail: Classic appearance: a smooth, shiny, dome-shaped nodule with a reddish or violaceous color, often with telangiectasias. May resemble a cyst, pyogenic granuloma, or basal cell carcinoma [7,19].

signs.name: Lymphadenopathy
signs.detail: Palpable regional lymph node involvement at presentation is common. Large meta-analyses show at least half of patients develop lymph node metastases [8-15].
signs.frequency: Occurs in >50% of patients over disease course

signs.name: In-transit metastases
signs.detail: Cutaneous or subcutaneous nodules between the primary tumor and the draining nodal basin, may be palpable or visible [12,66].
signs.frequency: Less common; reported in some series

atypical_or_missed: MCC is frequently misdiagnosed as benign cysts, lipomas, pyogenic granulomas, or other non-melanoma skin cancers (squamous cell carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma) due to its non-distinctive appearance [7,19,32,153].

Combined tumors containing both MCC and other malignancies (most commonly squamous cell carcinoma, but also basal cell carcinoma, melanoma, actinic keratosis, Bowen disease) occur in a subset of cases and may be mistaken for pure squamous cell carcinoma [29-32,61,85,92,105-113].
Epidermotropic MCC (with intraepidermal involvement) can be misinterpreted as a lymphoproliferative disorder or melanoma in situ [96,108-110,112,113,174].
MCC of unknown primary presenting as isolated lymph node metastasis without a detectable skin lesion is a recognized presentation with better prognosis than known primary [12,41,175-178].
In immunosuppressed patients (e.g., solid organ transplant, CLL, HIV), MCC may present atypically, with more aggressive growth and multifocal lesions [33-39].

red_flags: Rapidly growing nodule on sun-exposed skin (especially head/neck, extremities) with a latency of weeks to months [7].
Firm, red-to-purple dome-shaped nodule with telangiectasias, particularly >1 cm in diameter [19,22].
Presence of immunosuppression: solid organ transplant, chronic lymphocytic leukemia, HIV, or other causes of chronic T-cell immunosuppression [33-39].
Age >50 years; incidence increases with age, with most patients older than 65 [4,7,8,13,19].
History of prior or concurrent non-melanoma skin cancer, melanoma, or other cancers (MCC patients have increased risk of second primary malignancies) [19,23,35,91,94,97-104].
Palpable lymphadenopathy or in-transit nodules in the drainage basin of a suspicious skin lesion [12,66].
History of extensive sun exposure or residence in high UV index regions [26-32].
Positive sentinel lymph node biopsy or imaging evidence of occult nodal/distant metastases discovered during staging [19,50,83].
Rising serum Merkel cell polyomavirus (MCPyV) oncoprotein antibody titers (in seropositive patients) or new appearance of ctDNA positivity [55,122].
MCC diagnosed in patients <50 years of age (germline mutations in cancer-associated genes found in ~20%) [116].

diagnostic.test: Punch, shave, or excisional biopsy of suspicious skin lesion
diagnostic.rationale: Initial tissue diagnosis with adequate sampling [MCC-1].
diagnostic.key_findings: Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining shows small round cells with sparse cytoplasm, abundant mitoses, and dense core granules. Immunohistochemistry (IHC) confirmation required [MCC-A].

diagnostic.rationale: To distinguish MCC from other small round cell tumors, especially metastatic small cell lung cancer [MCC-A].
diagnostic.key_findings: CK20 positivity (membranous and/or paranuclear dot-like) and TTF-1 negativity are usually sufficient. If atypical pattern, use AE1/3 keratin (dot-like), at least one neuroendocrine marker (synaptophysin, neurofilament, INSM1, chromogranin, CD56, NSE), and/or MCPyV T-antigen (CM2B4) [MCC-A, 5,6,160,162-165].

diagnostic.test: Sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) with appropriate immunopanel
diagnostic.rationale: Most reliable tool to identify subclinical nodal disease. Should be performed prior to or at time of definitive excision [MCC-2, MCC-C].
diagnostic.key_findings: SLN positivity in 30-40% of clinically node-negative patients. Pathologic evaluation requires multiple levels (≥2) with H&E and at least one IHC stain (CK20 or pancytokeratin) [MCC-A, 19,46,49,94,127,180,181].

diagnostic.test: Fine-needle aspiration or core biopsy of palpable lymph nodes
diagnostic.rationale: Confirmation of nodal involvement in clinically N+ disease [MCC-4].
diagnostic.key_findings: Effective for verifying MCC in palpable nodes (>6 mm). Not appropriate for detection of clinically occult micrometastases [195-198].

diagnostic.test: Excisional biopsy of lymph node (if FNA/core negative but high suspicion)
diagnostic.rationale: Confirm negative initial biopsy if clinical suspicion remains high [MCC-4].

staging.test: Complete skin and lymph node examination
staging.rationale: Full skin inspection and palpation of all nodal basins to detect primary, in-transit, nodal, or distant cutaneous metastases [MCC-1].
staging.indication: All patients at initial presentation and at follow-up visits

staging.test: Whole-body FDG-PET/CT (preferred) or CT with contrast of chest/abdomen/pelvis (plus neck if primary on head/neck, and brain MRI if clinical suspicion of brain metastases)
staging.rationale: Imaging for staging of most MCC; occult metastatic disease detected in 12-20% of patients without suspicious H&P findings [151]. FDG-PET/CT has high sensitivity (90%) and specificity (98%) per meta-analysis [140]. May also help rule out skin metastasis from non-cutaneous primary neuroendocrine carcinoma (e.g., SCLC) [MCC-1].
staging.indication: Recommended for staging of most MCC; especially important for patients with clinically N0 disease to detect occult metastases. Whole-body FDG-PET/CT preferred at initial workup [MCC-1].

staging.rationale: Gold standard for staging the nodal basin in clinically N0 disease; impacts regional control and provides prognostic information [MCC-2, MCC-C].
staging.indication: All patients with clinically node-negative, localized MCC who are surgical candidates

staging.test: CT or MRI of brain with and without contrast
staging.rationale: Best imaging tool for rare MCC brain metastases, though most Panel members reserve for patients with neurological symptoms or widespread systemic disease [148-150,152].
staging.indication: Clinical suspicion of brain metastases or direct extension (e.g., head/neck primary with skull base involvement)

staging.rationale: Optional; may identify somatostatin receptor-expressing metastases for potential octreotide therapy [MCC-D, 317].
staging.indication: Consider if planning somatostatin analog therapy for advanced disease (category 2B)

biomarkers.test: Merkel cell polyomavirus (MCPyV) oncoprotein antibody testing
biomarkers.rationale: Prognostic and surveillance tool. Seronegative at diagnosis associated with higher recurrence risk. Seropositive patients with rising titers may have early recurrence [55,56,117-119].
biomarkers.when: Baseline testing within 3 months of treatment; surveillance every 3 months for seropositive patients [MCC-1, MCC-6].
biomarkers.mandatory: No, but recommended at initial workup and for surveillance

biomarkers.rationale: Assesses disease burden in both virus-positive and virus-negative MCC; becomes positive prior to or at time of clinically evident recurrence [122].
biomarkers.when: Baseline (requires sufficient tumor tissue; consider additional biopsy if core biopsy only). Surveillance every 3 months [MCC-1, MCC-6].
biomarkers.mandatory: No, but increasingly used; tissue-informed testing requires adequate tumor sample

biomarkers.test: Genetic counseling for germline mutations in cancer-associated genes
biomarkers.rationale: Mutations found in ~20% of patients <50 years of age [116].
biomarkers.when: Consider for patients <50 years at diagnosis [MCC-1].

do_not_miss: Biopsy with adequate tissue for both H&E and IHC (CK20, TTF-1, neuroendocrine markers) [MCC-A]
Sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) for clinically N0 disease – most reliable staging tool for subclinical nodal disease [MCC-2, MCC-C]
Whole-body FDG-PET/CT or appropriate CT imaging as baseline staging – can upstage 12-20% [151]
MCPyV oncoprotein antibody testing and ctDNA testing – both can aid surveillance and early detection of recurrence [55,122]
Assessment of immunosuppression status – poor prognostic factor; consider minimizing immunosuppression [MCC-1, 40,43,46,47,114,115]
Multidisciplinary consultation at a center with specialized expertise – recommended for all patients [MCC-1, MCC-3, MCC-4, MCC-5]
avoid: Avoid using only a core biopsy without collecting additional tumor tissue for tissue-informed ctDNA testing (insufficient tissue may negate usefulness of ctDNA surveillance) [MCC-1]
Avoid routine use of fine-needle aspiration (FNA) for detection of clinically occult lymph node metastases – FNA may miss small micrometastases; excisional biopsy or SLNB preferred [196]
Avoid omitting SLNB in surgically resectable clinically N0 MCC – strong prognostic and management implications [MCC-2]
Avoid using MRI brain routinely for all MCC patients without clinical suspicion – low yield in asymptomatic patients [152]
Avoid adjuvant systemic therapy outside of a clinical trial for primary resectable disease (no proven survival benefit) [MCC-D 2 of 5]

staging.system: AJCC 8th edition (2017) TNM staging system for Merkel cell carcinoma [12,66]

t_stages.description: Primary tumor cannot be assessed (e.g., curetted)
Maximum clinical tumor diameter >2 cm but ≤5 cm
Primary tumor invades fascia, muscle, cartilage, or bone (extracutaneous extension)

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be clinically assessed (e.g., previously removed for another reason, or because of body habitus)
No regional lymph node metastasis detected on clinical and/or radiologic examination
In-transit metastasis (discontinuous from primary tumor; located between primary tumor and draining regional nodal basin, or distal to the primary tumor) without lymph node metastasis
In-transit metastasis (discontinuous from primary tumor; located between primary tumor and draining regional nodal basin, or distal to the primary tumor) with lymph node metastasis
Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed (e.g., previously removed for another reason or not removed for pathological evaluation)
No regional lymph node metastasis detected on pathological evaluation

Metastasis in regional lymph node(s). Subcategories: pN1a(sn) = clinically occult metastasis identified only by sentinel lymph node biopsy; pN1a = clinically occult metastasis following lymph node dissection; pN1b = clinically and/or radiologically detected metastasis, microscopically confirmed
In-transit metastasis without lymph node metastasis
In-transit metastasis with lymph node metastasis

m_stages.description: No distant metastasis detected on clinical and/or radiologic examination

Distant metastasis detected on clinical and/or radiologic examination. Subcategories: M1a = metastasis to distant skin, distant subcutaneous tissue, or distant lymph node(s); M1b = metastasis to lung; M1c = metastasis to all other visceral sites
Distant metastasis microscopically confirmed (with same subcategories: pM1a, pM1b, pM1c)

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: In situ disease, no nodal or distant involvement
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Not specified separately; overall survival for MCC varies from 41-77% by stage [2,10,13,18-23]
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative – local excision with margin assessment

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Small primary tumor (≤2 cm), no nodal or distant disease
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: 5-year relative survival approximately 75-80% (estimated from NCDB data [12])
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative – surgery (excision + SLNB) ± adjuvant RT if risk factors

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Primary tumor >2 cm but ≤5 cm (T2) or >5 cm (T3), no nodal or distant disease
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative – surgery + adjuvant RT often indicated due to higher risk features

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Primary tumor invades fascia, muscle, cartilage, or bone; no nodal or distant disease
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative – surgery + adjuvant RT; may require multidisciplinary approach

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Regional nodal involvement (N1-3) without distant metastases; includes in-transit disease [12,66]
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative – node dissection + RT preferred; systemic therapy may be considered

stage_groups.criteria: T1-T4 N1a(sn) or N1a M0; or T0 N1b M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Clinically occult nodal metastasis (identified by SLNB or LND) or nodal metastasis from unknown primary with microscopic confirmation
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative – surgery (node dissection or RT) ± systemic therapy (clinical trial preferred)

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Clinically detected nodal metastasis (N1b), in-transit with or without nodal involvement (N2-N3)
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative intent but high risk – node dissection + RT preferred; strong consideration of systemic therapy/clinical trial

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Distant metastases (skin, subcutaneous tissue, lymph nodes, lung, other visceral sites)
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative – systemic therapy (immunotherapy preferred), RT, surgery for oligometastases, best supportive care

staging_pearls: Clinical N0 disease requires SLNB for accurate pathological staging; false-negative SLNB occurs in 5-12% of cases [46,50,180].
In-transit metastasis (N2/N3) is defined as discontinuous from primary tumor, located between primary tumor and draining nodal basin or distal to primary tumor [12,66].
Pathological staging (pN) refines nodal categories with subcategories pN1a(sn), pN1a, pN1b based on method of detection and clinical presentation.
M-stage subcategories M1a, M1b, M1c have prognostic significance; brain metastases are rare but MRI indicated when clinical suspicion [148-150].
Patients with unknown primary MCC (T0 N1b M0) have better prognosis than those with synchronous known primary [12,41,175-178].
The AJCC 8th edition is based on analysis of 9387 patients from NCDB with median follow-up 28.2 months [12].
Tumor thickness (Breslow, mm) is strongly encouraged to report but is not part of T staging; it correlates with SLN positivity and survival [49,76,83-85].
Stage IIA includes T2 and T3 tumors; outcomes for T2 vs T3 may differ but grouped due to similar survival curves [10,12].

management_principles.overview: Merkel cell carcinoma (MCC) is a rare but aggressive cutaneous neuroendocrine malignancy requiring a multidisciplinary, multimodal approach to optimize outcomes. The treatment philosophy centers on accurate staging, complete surgical resection when feasible, and judicious use of adjuvant or definitive radiation therapy (RT) and systemic therapy, particularly immune checkpoint inhibitors. Given the high propensity for locoregional recurrence and distant metastasis, the NCCN Panel strongly recommends multidisciplinary consultation at a center with specialized expertise for all stages of disease (MCC-1, MCC-3, MCC-4, MCC-5) [1-3]. Immunosuppression is a key risk factor for poor outcomes; therefore, immunosuppressive treatments should be minimized as clinically feasible, and more frequent follow-up is indicated for immunocompromised patients (MCC-1) [4,5]. For patients younger than 50 years, genetic counseling to evaluate for germline mutations in cancer-associated genes is recommended, as such mutations are reported in approximately 20% of this age group (MCC-1) [6]. The overall treatment plan integrates surgery, RT, and systemic therapy based on clinical and pathologic stage, resectability, patient fitness, and tumor biology.

by_intent.patient_group: Clinical N0, local MCC only, surgically resectable
by_intent.approach: Primary treatment consists of surgical excision with 1–2 cm margins (or Mohs/PDEMA in selected circumstances) plus sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB). For microscopically positive margins, adjuvant RT is preferred over re-excision. For SLN-negative patients with no adverse risk factors, observation is sufficient; with one or more adverse risk factors (LVI, immunosuppression, tumor >1 cm, close/positive margins, positive SLNB, head/neck site), adjuvant RT to the primary site is recommended (category 2A). For SLN-positive patients, baseline imaging is obtained, and additional treatment includes RT to the nodal basin, node dissection, or both. Node dissection with adjuvant RT is indicated for multiple involved nodes or extranodal extension (MCC-2, MCC-2A) [7-9].

by_intent.intent: Curative intent for locally advanced disease not amenable to upfront surgery/RT
by_intent.patient_group: Clinical N0, locally advanced MCC when curative surgery and RT are not feasible
by_intent.approach: Multidisciplinary consultation is required. For surgical candidates, neoadjuvant nivolumab may be considered (category 2A, useful in certain circumstances). After neoadjuvant therapy, adequate responders proceed to excision and SLNB per resectable pathway; those with progression are offered RT or surgery for local control versus systemic therapy. For nonsurgical candidates (due to comorbidities or tumor characteristics), discussion of RT for durable local control versus systemic therapy is recommended (MCC-3) [10].

by_intent.patient_group: Clinical N+ (regional MCC/in-transit) without distant metastases
by_intent.approach: Management of the draining nodal basin in N+ disease: preferred treatment is node dissection plus RT. Alternative options include node dissection alone, RT alone, clinical trial, or consideration of systemic therapy. For in-transit disease, biopsy confirmation is followed by clinical trial, surgery, RT, systemic therapy, or additional local considerations (T-VEC, hyperthermic isolated limb infusion/perfusion) if curative surgery/RT not feasible (MCC-4) [11,12].

Multidisciplinary consultation at specialized center. Clinical trial preferred. Treatment options include systemic therapy (PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors preferred), RT for symptom control, and surgery for limited metastases in highly selective circumstances. Best supportive care is always appropriate (MCC-5) [13].

Follow-up every 3–6 months for 3 years, then every 6–12 months. For local/regional recurrence: clinical trial, systemic therapy, RT, surgery, or best supportive care. In-transit recurrence: manage per in-transit pathway. Disseminated recurrence: treat as M1 disease. Serum MCPyV oncoprotein antibody and ctDNA surveillance every 3 months may aid early detection (MCC-6) [14,15].

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Multidisciplinary consultation at a center with specialized expertise is recommended for all patients at initial workup (MCC-1) and for those with locally advanced disease (MCC-3), clinical N+ disease (MCC-4), and M1 disease (MCC-5). The team should include dermatologists, surgical oncologists, radiation oncologists, medical oncologists, pathologists, and radiologists experienced in MCC management. This collaboration ensures appropriate staging, treatment sequencing, and consideration of clinical trials.
management_principles.performance_status_guide: The NCCN guideline does not provide a specific performance status (PS) scale or score for treatment selection. However, PS is implicitly considered when determining surgical candidacy. For nonsurgical candidates due to comorbidities, definitive RT or systemic therapy is discussed. For patients with M1 disease, best supportive care is an option regardless of PS. The decision to use systemic therapy, especially immunotherapy, should balance potential benefit with the risk of immune-related adverse events. Clinician and patient education on immunotherapy toxicities is critical (MCC-D 1 of 5) [16].

management_pathways.pathway_id: Clinical N0 local MCC surgically resectable
management_pathways.title: Primary and Additional Treatment of Clinical N0 Disease: Local MCC Only, Surgically Resectable
branching_basis: margin status (clear, microscopically positive, grossly positive)
presence of adverse risk factors (LVI, immunosuppression, tumor >1 cm, close/positive margins, positive SLNB, head/neck site)

nodes.criteria: SLN negative, clear margins, no adverse risk factors

options.label: Observation
options.recommendation: For SLN-negative patients with clear margins and no adverse risk factors, observation of the primary site and nodal basin is recommended (category 2A).

nodes.criteria: SLN negative, clear margins, one or more adverse risk factors present

options.label: Adjuvant RT to primary site
options.recommendation: Adjuvant RT to the primary tumor bed is recommended (category 2A). Doses: 50–56 Gy for negative margins, 56–60 Gy for microscopically positive margins, 60 Gy for grossly positive margins (conventional fractionation). Hypofractionation options exist for selected patients (MCC-B 2 of 6) [17].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant RT indicated due to adverse risk factors conferring higher local recurrence risk.

nodes.criteria: SLN negative, microscopically positive margins (with or without adverse risk factors)

options.label: Adjuvant RT to primary site
options.recommendation: Adjuvant RT is preferred over re-excision (category 2A). If adverse risk factors present and RT planned, narrower clinical margins (aiming for clear or microscopically positive) may be considered to reduce morbidity (MCC-2, MCC-2A) [7].

options.label: Re-excision
options.recommendation: Re-excision may be considered if adjuvant RT is not planned or if margins are grossly positive and further resection possible (MCC-2).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant RT preferred; re-excision is an alternative in selected settings.

options.label: Baseline imaging if not already performed
options.recommendation: Whole-body FDG-PET/CT preferred; CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (plus neck CT if head/neck primary) and brain MRI if clinical suspicion (MCC-2A) [18].

options.label: RT to nodal basin
options.recommendation: RT to the nodal basin is an option (category 2A). Dose: 50–56 Gy for SLNB without node dissection; 50–60 Gy after node dissection with multiple involved nodes or ENE (MCC-B 4 of 6) [19].

options.label: Node dissection
options.recommendation: Node dissection is an alternative; if performed, observation is acceptable if only single node involved without ENE. Adjuvant RT is recommended for multiple involved nodes or ENE (MCC-2) [9].

adjuvant_logic.summary: For SLN-positive disease, additional nodal treatment (RT or dissection) is indicated; node dissection + RT preferred for high-risk features.

management_pathways.pathway_id: Clinical N0 locally advanced MCC not feasible for curative surgery and RT
management_pathways.title: Primary and Additional Treatment of Clinical N0 Disease: Locally Advanced MCC When Curative Surgery and Adjuvant RT Are Not Feasible

nodes.criteria: Nonsurgical candidate due to comorbidities or tumor characteristics

options.label: Definitive RT for durable local control
options.recommendation: Discuss RT for durable local control versus systemic therapy (category 2A). Doses: 56–60 Gy conventional fractionation for clinically evident tumor; hypofractionation options available (MCC-B 2 of 6) [17].

options.label: Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy options per locally advanced disease (see systemic therapy section) (MCC-3).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Primary treatment is non-surgical; no adjuvant therapy defined.

options.label: Neoadjuvant nivolumab
options.recommendation: Neoadjuvant nivolumab may be considered (category 2A, useful in certain circumstances) for patients who are surgical candidates (MCC-3) [10].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Neoadjuvant therapy given; subsequent treatment depends on response.

options.label: Excision with 1–2 cm margins or Mohs/PDEMA plus SLNB
options.recommendation: Proceed to primary and additional treatment as per resectable local MCC pathway (MCC-2) (MCC-3).

adjuvant_logic.summary: After resection, additional treatment per MCC-2 based on SLN and margin status.

options.label: RT or surgery for local control
options.recommendation: Discuss RT or surgery for local control versus systemic therapy (MCC-3).

options.label: Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Alternative systemic therapy options (e.g., anti-PD-1/PD-L1 or combination) may be considered (MCC-3).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Progression management focuses on local vs. systemic options.

management_pathways.title: Treatment, Clinical Findings, Additional Treatment, and Follow-up of Clinical N+ Disease: Regional MCC/In-transit
branching_basis: Nodal basin biopsy result (positive or negative)
Presence of distant metastases (M0 vs. M1)

nodes.criteria: Positive FNA/core biopsy of draining nodal basin, M0

options.label: Node dissection plus RT (preferred)
options.recommendation: Node dissection followed by RT to the nodal basin (category 2A). Adjuvant RT dose: 50–60 Gy for multiple involved nodes or ENE (MCC-B 4 of 6) [19].

options.label: Node dissection alone
options.recommendation: Node dissection alone is an option (category 2A).

options.label: RT alone
options.recommendation: Definitive RT to nodal basin (category 2A). Dose: 60 Gy for clinically evident lymphadenopathy without dissection; 46–50 Gy for subclinical disease (MCC-B 4 of 6) [20].

options.label: Clinical trial
options.recommendation: Enrollment in a clinical trial is preferred if available (MCC-4).

options.label: Consider systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy may be considered as part of initial management (MCC-4).

adjuvant_logic.summary: After node dissection, adjuvant RT indicated for multiple involved nodes or ENE; otherwise observation or RT alone if no dissection.

nodes.criteria: Negative FNA/core biopsy of draining nodal basin

options.label: Radiographic surveillance
options.recommendation: Continue radiographic surveillance per clinical N0 pathway (MCC-4).

options.label: Excisional biopsy if high clinical suspicion
options.recommendation: Excisional biopsy may be considered to confirm negative initial biopsy (MCC-4).

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant therapy if nodal basin negative.

nodes.criteria: Positive nodal basin with distant metastases (M1)

options.label: Follow M1 pathway
options.recommendation: Manage as disseminated disease per MCC-5 (MCC-4).

adjuvant_logic.summary: M1 disease management is systemic therapy-focused.

options.label: Clinical trial
options.recommendation: Clinical trial preferred if available (MCC-4).

options.label: Surgery
options.recommendation: Surgical resection of in-transit lesions may be considered (MCC-4).

options.label: RT
options.recommendation: RT for local control (MCC-4).

options.label: Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy options per regional disease (MCC-D 3 of 5) [21].

options.label: Additional local considerations if curative surgery/RT not feasible
options.recommendation: T-VEC (intralesional talimogene laherparepvec) for palliation of injectable sites (category 2A) and hyperthermic isolated limb infusion/perfusion (category 2A) may be useful (MCC-4) [22,23].

adjuvant_logic.summary: In-transit disease management is individualized; no standard adjuvant regimen.

options.label: Clinical trial preferred if available
options.recommendation: Enrollment in a clinical trial is the preferred option when available (MCC-5).

options.label: Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: First-line systemic therapy with anti-PD-1/PD-L1 agents: avelumab, nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or retifanlimab-dlwr (all category 2A preferred). Ipilimumab + nivolumab is other recommended, particularly for PD-1 refractory disease (category 2A). If contraindicated or progressed, chemotherapy options: carboplatin, carboplatin/etoposide, cisplatin, cisplatin/etoposide, CAV, topotecan; also ipilimumab, octreotide LAR (category 2B), pazopanib (category 2B), T-VEC for injectable palliation (category 2B). NGS may reveal actionable targets (MCC-D 4 of 5) [13,21,24-26].

options.label: RT
options.recommendation: RT can be used for symptom palliation or local control (MCC-5).

options.label: Surgery
options.recommendation: Under highly selective circumstances, resection of limited metastases may be considered after multidisciplinary consultation (MCC-5) [13].

options.label: Best supportive care
options.recommendation: Best supportive care (NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care) is always an appropriate option (MCC-5).

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant therapy; treatment is palliative or disease-modifying in metastatic setting.

management_pathways.title: Follow-up, Recurrence, and Treatment
branching_basis: Type of recurrence (local, locally advanced, regional, in-transit, disseminated)

nodes.criteria: Local, locally advanced, or regional recurrence

options.label: Clinical trial preferred if available
options.recommendation: Consider clinical trial if available (MCC-6).

options.label: Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy options as per M1 disease (MCC-6).

options.label: RT
options.recommendation: RT for locoregional control (MCC-6).

options.label: Surgery
options.recommendation: Surgery for resectable recurrence (MCC-6).

options.label: Best supportive care
options.recommendation: Best supportive care (MCC-6).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Treatment of recurrence is based on extent and prior therapies; no standard adjuvant post-recurrence.

options.label: Manage as clinical N+ in-transit disease
options.recommendation: Follow the in-transit disease pathway (MCC-4) including multidisciplinary consultation, clinical trial, surgery, RT, systemic therapy, and additional local considerations (MCC-6).

adjuvant_logic.summary: In-transit recurrence managed per initial in-transit pathway.

options.label: Treatment of M1 disease
options.recommendation: Follow M1 disseminated disease pathway (MCC-5) (MCC-6).

items.text: Complete history and physical examination, including complete skin and lymph node examination
items.recommendation: Essential for initial evaluation and staging (MCC-1) [1].

items.text: Multidisciplinary consultation at a center with specialized expertise
items.recommendation: Strongly recommended for all patients at initial workup (MCC-1) [3].

items.text: Whole-body FDG-PET/CT (preferred at initial workup) OR CT with contrast of chest, abdomen, and pelvis (plus neck CT if primary on head/neck)
items.recommendation: Imaging is encouraged for staging of most cases because occult metastatic disease is detected in 12–20% of patients without suspicious H&P findings. FDG-PET/CT is more sensitive for occult metastases (MCC-1) [18].

items.text: MRI of brain with and without contrast
items.recommendation: If clinical suspicion of brain metastases or direct extension (MCC-1) [27].

items.text: Quantitation of serum Merkel cell polyomavirus (MCPyV) oncoprotein antibodies
items.recommendation: May be considered as part of initial workup. Seronegative patients have higher risk of recurrence. Rising titer may indicate recurrence. Baseline testing within 3 months of treatment. Surveillance every 3 months (MCC-1) [14].
Can assess disease burden in both virus-positive and virus-negative MCC. Typically becomes positive prior to or at time of clinically evident recurrence. Surveillance every 3 months (MCC-1) [15].

items.text: Additional tumor tissue collection if diagnosis made only with core biopsy
items.recommendation: Consider collecting additional tissue required for tissue-informed ctDNA testing prior to immunotherapy or RT (MCC-1) [15].

items.text: Biopsy with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and immunopanel
items.recommendation: Initial diagnosis confirmed by H&E; immunopanel should include CK20 and TTF-1 (usually sufficient). Additional markers (AE1/AE3, synaptophysin, neurofilament, INSM1, chromogranin, CD56, NSE, MCPyV T antigen) may be used if atypical staining (MCC-A) [28].

items.text: Synoptic reporting preferred for primary tumor pathology
items.recommendation: Minimum elements: largest tumor diameter, peripheral and deep margin status, LVI, extracutaneous extension. Additional encouraged: thickness (Breslow), TILs (not identified, brisk, non-brisk), growth pattern (nodular/infiltrative), presence of second malignancy (e.g., concurrent SCC) (MCC-A) [29].

SLNB evaluation requires microscopic evaluation of entire node, multiple levels (≥2) including H&E and at least one IHC stain (e.g., CK20). Report number of LN involved, size of largest metastatic deposit, presence of ENE (MCC-A) [30].
Consider genetic counseling to evaluate for germline mutations in cancer-associated genes, reported in approximately 20% of patients <50 years (MCC-1) [6].

For immunocompromised patients, consider modification or reduction of immunosuppression as clinically feasible in consultation with managing physician. More frequent follow-up may be indicated (MCC-1) [4,5].

surgery.role: Surgery is the primary treatment modality for most MCC and is required for accurate pathological staging of both the primary lesion and regional disease. The goals are to obtain histologically negative margins when clinically feasible while balancing morbidity, and to facilitate appropriate adjuvant therapy. Surgery includes excision of the primary tumor and sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB), with or without completion lymph node dissection depending on nodal status.
principles: Obtain histologically negative margins when clinically feasible; surgical margins should be balanced with morbidity of surgery (MCC-C).

Coordinate surgical management so that SLNB is performed prior to or at the time of definitive excision, especially for head and neck primaries where prior resection/reconstruction may alter lymphatic drainage (MCC-C) [31].

If adjuvant RT is planned, narrow excision margins are likely sufficient; if adjuvant RT may not be indicated, perform wide excision with 1- to 2-cm margins to investing fascia of muscle or pericranium when clinically feasible and consistent with reconstruction and RT goals (MCC-C) [7].

Techniques for more exhaustive histologic margin assessment (Mohs or other forms of PDEMA) may be considered provided they do not interfere with SLNB; if SLNB not performed concurrently, perform SLNB prior to definitive excision (MCC-C) [32].

Reconstruction involving extensive undermining or tissue movement should be delayed until negative histologic margins are verified and SLNB performed if indicated. Closure should allow for expeditious initiation of RT (e.g., primary closure, avoiding extensive tissue movement) (MCC-C) [7].

procedures.name: Wide local excision (WLE)
procedures.approach: Standard excision with 1- to 2-cm clinical margins to investing fascia or pericranium.
procedures.indications: Primary MCC with clinically node-negative disease, especially if adjuvant RT is not planned. Also for primary tumor in regional disease setting.
procedures.key_complications: Wound healing issues, need for skin grafting or flap reconstruction, potential delay to adjuvant RT.

procedures.name: Mohs micrographic surgery
procedures.approach: Layer-by-layer excision with complete peripheral and deep margin assessment (PDEMA).
procedures.indications: Selected cases, especially in cosmetically sensitive areas or when tissue preservation is important. May achieve high local control rates. Requires coordination with SLNB timing.
procedures.key_complications: Potential for incomplete margin assessment if SLNB performed separately; need to submit central specimen for staging parameters if preoperative biopsy insufficient.

procedures.name: Sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB)
procedures.approach: Lymphoscintigraphy and intraoperative mapping to identify sentinel nodes; excisional biopsy with pathological evaluation using H&E and IHC (CK20, pancytokeratins).
procedures.indications: All patients with clinically node-negative, surgically resectable MCC (category 2A). Important staging tool that impacts regional control for SLN-positive patients.
procedures.key_complications: False-negative rate 17–21%; complex drainage patterns in head/neck; risk of lymphedema.

procedures.name: Lymph node dissection (LND)
procedures.approach: Completion dissection of the nodal basin (e.g., axillary, inguinal, cervical) after positive SLNB or clinically evident nodal disease.
procedures.indications: Positive SLNB (especially if multiple nodes or ENE); clinically N+ disease confirmed by biopsy. Preferred combined with RT for high-risk features.

surgery.resectability_criteria: The guideline defines 'local MCC only' as disease limited to the primary tumor with no in-transit, nodal, or distant metastases (MCC-2A). Resectability is determined by multidisciplinary assessment: tumor characteristics (size, location, involvement of critical structures) and patient comorbidities. For locally advanced disease where curative surgery and adjuvant RT are not feasible due to comorbidities or tumor characteristics, patients are considered nonsurgical candidates and offered definitive RT or systemic therapy (MCC-3). Under highly selective circumstances, resection of limited metastases in M1 disease may be considered after multidisciplinary consultation (MCC-5) [13].

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy plays a critical role in both curative and palliative management of MCC. It is used as adjuvant therapy after surgery to reduce locoregional recurrence, as definitive treatment for patients who are not surgical candidates or refuse surgery, and for palliation of symptoms. The NCCN Panel emphasizes expeditious initiation of adjuvant RT after surgery as soon as wound healing permits, as delays >7–8 weeks are associated with worse outcomes (MCC-B 1 of 6) [33].
principles: Limited evidence supports dosing recommendations for MCC; dose ranges are based on clinical practice at NCCN Member Institutions and clinical evidence from other skin cancers (MCC-B 1 of 6).
Recommendations for external beam RT (EBRT) apply to both photons and electrons.
Image-guided radiation therapy (IGRT) is considered best practice when treating with IMRT, proton beam, or 3D conformal RT; IGRT for other skin cancer RT techniques is unnecessary (MCC-B 1 of 6).
Application of bolus over the primary tumor should be considered to ensure full dose to the surface; surface dosimeters should be considered to verify delivered dose (MCC-B 1 of 6).
Radiation treatments should be given by a practicing radiation oncologist with radiation physics support to meet quality assurance and dosimetric constraints (MCC-B 1 of 6).

For primary tumor site, wide margins (5 cm) should be used when clinically feasible, with consideration of anatomic constraints (e.g., eye, mouth). If electron beam, energy and prescription isodose must ensure adequate dose to lateral and deep margins (MCC-B 2 of 6).

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant RT to primary tumor – conventional fractionation
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 50–56 Gy for negative margins; 56–60 Gy for microscopically positive margins; 60 Gy for grossly positive margins (if further resection not possible).
dose_frameworks.indication: Postoperative RT for patients with ≥1 risk factors for local recurrence (LVI, immunosuppression, primary >1 cm, close/positive margins, positive SLNB, head/neck site). In subsets at high risk (e.g., chronic profound immune suppression), up to 66 Gy may be considered (MCC-B 2 of 6, MCC-B 3 of 6) [17].

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant RT to primary tumor – hypofractionation
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 44–50 Gy (2.2–2.5 Gy x 20 fractions); 30–35 Gy (3–3.5 Gy x 10 fractions); 8 Gy x 1 fraction (for selected low-risk patients).
dose_frameworks.indication: Consider for patients who cannot tolerate conventional fractionation; avoid in patients at higher risk (e.g., immunosuppression) where data limited (MCC-B 2 of 6, MCC-B 3 of 6) [17].

dose_frameworks.name: Definitive RT to primary tumor (clinically evident) – conventional fractionation
dose_frameworks.indication: Unresectable tumor, surgery refused, or surgery would result in significant morbidity (MCC-B 2 of 6) [17].

dose_frameworks.name: Definitive RT to primary tumor – hypofractionation
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 50 Gy (2.5 Gy x 20); 45–51 Gy (3 Gy x 15–17); 40 Gy (4 Gy x 10); 30–35 Gy (6–7 Gy x 5, non-consecutive); 24 Gy (8 Gy x 3, non-consecutive).
dose_frameworks.indication: Alternative for patients with limited access or comorbidities; caution with fractions >5 Gy/fraction when targets involve cartilage or bone (MCC-B 2 of 6, MCC-B 3 of 6) [17].

dose_frameworks.name: Palliative RT to primary tumor or metastases
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 30 Gy (3 Gy x 10); 20 Gy (4 Gy x 5); 8–24 Gy (8 Gy x 1–3 fractions).
dose_frameworks.indication: Symptom control; durable palliation likely higher with 8 Gy x 3 fractions (MCC-B 2 of 6, MCC-B 3 of 6) [17].

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant RT to regional nodes – conventional fractionation
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 50–56 Gy (SLNB without LND, SLN positive); 50–60 Gy (after LND with multiple involved nodes and/or ENE).
dose_frameworks.indication: For SLN-positive disease: RT to nodal basin if no dissection; after dissection for multiple nodes/ENE. RT to nodal basin may be considered in certain high-risk scenarios (compromised SLNB, high false-negative risk, profound immunosuppression) (MCC-B 4 of 6) [19,34].

dose_frameworks.name: Definitive RT to regional nodes – conventional fractionation
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 60 Gy (no SLNB or LND, clinically evident lymphadenopathy); 46–50 Gy (clinically node negative but at risk for subclinical disease).
dose_frameworks.indication: For patients with clinically evident nodal disease who are not candidates for LND, or for elective nodal irradiation (MCC-B 4 of 6) [20].

dose_frameworks.name: Hypofractionation and palliative RT for regional nodes
dose_frameworks.total_dose: Hypofractionation: 44–50 Gy (2.2–2.5 Gy x 20); 30–35 Gy (3–3.5 Gy x 10); 16 Gy (8 Gy x 2). Palliative: 30 Gy (3 Gy x 10); 20 Gy (4 Gy x 5); 8–24 Gy (8 Gy x 1–3).
dose_frameworks.schedule: Daily or as specified with caution for lymphedema risk.
dose_frameworks.indication: Hypofractionation for selected patients; palliative for symptom control. Use caution in patients with existing lymphedema or high risk (high BMI, groin basin, current smoker). Hypofractionation generally reserved for older patients or those unable to come for protracted course (MCC-B 4 of 6, MCC-B 5 of 6) [17].

approaches.name: Adjuvant RT (postoperative)
approaches.dose_fractionation: Conventional fractionation (1.8–2 Gy/fx) as above for primary and nodal basins; hypofractionation alternatives.
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Not routinely recommended. Data do not support routine use of concurrent chemoradiation; may be considered on case-by-case basis (e.g., positive margins, tumor ≥3 cm) but survival benefit not clearly established (MCC-D 3 of 5) [21,35].
approaches.indication: Patients with ≥1 risk factors for local recurrence; SLN-positive disease; positive margins; after node dissection for multiple nodes/ENE.
approaches.key_trial: Multiple retrospective analyses and NCDB studies show improved locoregional control and survival with adjuvant RT in appropriate patients [7,19,20,33,35].
approaches.toxicities: Acute: dermatitis, fatigue, mucositis if head/neck. Late: fibrosis, lymphedema (especially nodal RT), secondary malignancy risk (small).

approaches.name: Definitive RT (without surgery)
approaches.dose_fractionation: Conventional fractionation 56–60 Gy (primary) or 60 Gy (nodes); hypofractionation options.
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Not standard; concurrent chemotherapy not supported by high-level evidence.
approaches.indication: Patients with unresectable disease, medical inoperability, or refusal of surgery. May provide good local control.
approaches.key_trial: Retrospective data: SEER analysis showed improved OS and DSS with RT alone vs. no treatment in non-surgical patients. In-field recurrence after definitive RT ~12% per site [20,36].
approaches.toxicities: Similar to adjuvant RT; higher doses may increase late effects.

approaches.name: Palliative RT
approaches.dose_fractionation: Various hypofractionated schedules (e.g., 8 Gy x 1, 20 Gy/5 fx, 30 Gy/10 fx).
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Not typically used; may be combined with systemic therapy in selected cases.
approaches.indication: Symptomatic metastases (bone pain, bleeding, obstruction, brain metastases). Durable palliation better with higher dose regimens (8 Gy x 3).
approaches.key_trial: No dedicated MCC trials; extrapolated from palliative RT for other solid tumors.
approaches.toxicities: Generally well tolerated; acute fatigue, skin reaction, depending on site.

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy for MCC has evolved significantly with the advent of immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs). Non-randomized trials demonstrate that rates of durable response are improved with PD-1/PD-L1 blockade compared with cytotoxic therapy, and safety profiles differ substantially. Chemotherapy, while associated with response rates of 40–60%, tends to have short durations and higher toxicity. The NCCN Panel emphasizes enrollment in clinical trials when available. Adjuvant systemic therapy outside a clinical trial is not recommended for primary resectable disease. For locally advanced unresectable, regional, and disseminated disease, ICIs are the preferred first-line approach. The following sections organize recommendations by stage and setting, with specific regimens and evidence.

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Adjuvant systemic therapy is not recommended outside of a clinical trial for primary resectable MCC. There is no evidence of survival benefit for adjuvant chemotherapy, and immunotherapy data in this setting are immature (MCC-D 2 of 5) [21].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Primary locally advanced (curative surgery and RT not feasible)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Both are category 2A preferred based on phase II data showing durable responses in advanced MCC. Avelumab: JAVELIN Merkel 200 part B (first-line) reported ORR 39.7%, median PFS 4.1 months, median OS 20.3 months, 4-year OS 38% [24,25,37]. Pembrolizumab: phase II first-line in advanced MCC (N=50) showed ORR 58%, median PFS 16.8 months, 3-year OS 59.4% [26,38,39].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Retifanlimab-dlwr (Other Recommended, category 2A): POD1UM-201 trial (chemo-naïve) showed ORR 46.2% [40]. Neoadjuvant Nivolumab (Useful in Certain Circumstances, category 2A): CheckMate 358 in resectable stage IIA–IV showed pCR 47.2% [10].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Recurrent locally advanced (curative surgery and RT not feasible)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Data extrapolated from advanced MCC trials.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: None for standard adjuvant; Neoadjuvant Nivolumab (Useful in Certain Circumstances) for select patients.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Adjuvant chemotherapy is not routinely recommended; no survival benefit in retrospective studies. Adjuvant immunotherapy not recommended outside clinical trial. Neoadjuvant nivolumab may be considered for surgical candidates (MCC-D 3 of 5) [21].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Primary regional, recurrent regional, or in-transit disease (if curative surgery and RT not feasible)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Avelumab, Nivolumab, Pembrolizumab, Retifanlimab-dlwr (all category 2A preferred)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Same ICI options as disseminated disease based on efficacy in advanced MCC. Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (Other Recommended, category 2A) is particularly indicated for PD-1 refractory disease [41,42]. For in-transit, T-VEC (intralesional talimogene laherparepvec) for palliation of injectable sites and hyperthermic isolated limb infusion/perfusion are Useful in Certain Circumstances (category 2A) [22,23].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: If anti-PD-(L)1 contraindicated or progressed: carboplatin, carboplatin/etoposide, cisplatin, cisplatin/etoposide, CAV (cyclophosphamide/doxorubicin+vincristine), ipilimumab monotherapy, octreotide LAR (if SSTR positive), pazopanib, topotecan, T-VEC. Carboplatin/etoposide and cisplatin/etoposide are for recurrent regional disease only (MCC-D 3 of 5) [21,43,44].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Avelumab, Nivolumab, Pembrolizumab, Retifanlimab-dlwr (all category 2A preferred)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: First-line ICI therapy is standard. Checkpoint inhibitors provide higher durability than chemotherapy. Nivolumab monotherapy in CheckMate 358 (treatment-naïve) showed ORR 71%, median PFS 21.3 months [45]. Avelumab as above. Pembrolizumab as above. Retifanlimab as above. Combination ipilimumab+nivolumab (Other Recommended) effective in ICI-refractory disease [41,42].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: If anti-PD-(L)1 contraindicated/progressed: same chemotherapy options as regional disease, but note that octreotide LAR, pazopanib, and T-VEC are category 2B for M1. NGS may reveal actionable targets (MCC-D 4 of 5) [13,24,43,44,46].

key_regimens.name: Avelumab
key_regimens.setting: First-line or subsequent therapy for locally advanced/metastatic MCC
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-related adverse events (irAEs): fatigue, infusion reaction, rash, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies. Grade 3–4 irAEs ~5% [24,25,37].

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab
key_regimens.setting: First-line or subsequent therapy for locally advanced/metastatic MCC
key_regimens.key_toxicities: irAEs similar to avelumab. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab (different dosing) [26,38,39].

key_regimens.name: Nivolumab
key_regimens.setting: First-line or subsequent therapy for locally advanced/metastatic MCC; also neoadjuvant setting
key_regimens.key_toxicities: irAEs. Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV (different dosing) [10,45].

key_regimens.name: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab
key_regimens.cycle_days: 21-day cycle for combination induction; nivolumab maintenance every 2–4 weeks
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Increased irAEs; grade 3–4 in 32–40% in phase II study [41,42].

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin + Etoposide
key_regimens.setting: Recurrent regional or M1 disease when immunotherapy contraindicated or after progression

key_regimens.name: T-VEC (Talimogene laherparepvec)
key_regimens.setting: Palliation of injectable sites in locoregional or metastatic MCC (especially in-transit or cutaneous metastases)

drugs.name: Talimogene laherparepvec
drugs.dose: Initial 10^6 PFU/mL, then 10^8 PFU/mL every 2 weeks

key_regimens.cycle_days: Continue until complete response or unacceptable toxicity
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Local injection site reactions, flu-like symptoms, rare visceral dissemination [22].

treatment_response_assessment.title: Follow-up, Surveillance, and Response Assessment
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Follow-up visits should begin immediately after treatment completion. Physical exam including complete skin and lymph node examination every 3–6 months for the first 3 years, then every 6–12 months thereafter. More frequent follow-up may be indicated for immunocompromised patients (MCC-6) [4,5].
response_logic: No standardized response criteria are specified for MCC; assessment is based on physical exam, imaging, and biomarkers. For patients on systemic therapy, standard RECIST 1.1 criteria are typically applied per clinical trial protocols.

For patients receiving neoadjuvant nivolumab, response is assessed radiographically and pathologically. Adequate response allows proceeding to surgery; progression leads to discussion of local control vs. alternative systemic therapy (MCC-3).

Serum MCPyV oncoprotein antibody testing: seronegative patients have higher recurrence risk. For seropositive patients, a rising titer may be an early indicator of recurrence. Baseline testing within 3 months of treatment; surveillance every 3 months (MCC-1, MCC-6) [14].

ctDNA assessment: can detect disease burden in both virus-positive and virus-negative MCC. Typically becomes positive prior to or at time of clinically evident recurrence. Surveillance every 3 months (MCC-1, MCC-6) [15].
imaging_recommendations: Routine imaging surveillance is recommended for patients at high risk of recurrence: advancing age, immunosuppression, advancing stage (II–IV), non-SLN metastases, MCPyV-negative status (MCC-6) [47].
Surveillance imaging typically involves diagnostic CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis with oral and IV contrast; neck CT often included if primary lesion was on head/neck (MCC-6).
Whole-body FDG-PET/CT may be indicated to evaluate for in-transit metastases if primary lesion is on extremity (MCC-6).
Imaging should also be performed as clinically indicated for new symptoms (e.g., adenopathy, organomegaly, neurologic signs) (MCC-6).
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: If recurrence is suspected on physical exam or imaging, tissue biopsy (FNA, core, or excisional) with appropriate immunopanel is recommended to confirm diagnosis and rule out second primary (MCC-4, MCC-6).
For local, locally advanced, or regional recurrence: treatment options include clinical trial (preferred), systemic therapy, RT, surgery, or best supportive care (MCC-6).
For in-transit recurrence: manage per in-transit disease pathway (multidisciplinary consultation, clinical trial, surgery, RT, systemic therapy, local considerations) (MCC-6).
For disseminated recurrence: treat as M1 disease (MCC-6).
Under highly selective circumstances, resection of limited metastases can be considered after multidisciplinary consultation (MCC-5) [13].
For patients who progress on anti-PD-(L)1 monotherapy, ipilimumab + nivolumab is particularly indicated as salvage therapy (MCC-D 3 of 4) [41,42].

prognosis.overall: Merkel cell carcinoma (MCC) is a rare and aggressive cutaneous neuroendocrine neoplasm with a high mortality rate, exceeding that of melanoma. The 5-year relative or MCC-specific survival rates range from 41% to 77%, depending on stage at presentation [2,10,13,18-23]. Approximately 63% of primary lesions grow rapidly within 3 months prior to diagnosis [7]. At least half of patients develop lymph node metastases, and nearly one third develop distant metastases [8-15]. Locoregional recurrence occurs in up to half of all cases [14-20].

by_stage.context: Range depending on stage at presentation; specific stage-level figures are not provided in the extracted guideline but referenced in multiple studies [2,10,13,18-23].
Surgery ± adjuvant RT is mainstay; risk factors for recurrence include LVI, immunosuppression, tumor >1 cm, positive margins, head/neck site, positive SLNB [MCC-2A].
Nodal involvement carries worse prognosis; treatment includes node dissection, RT, and systemic therapy [MCC-4, MS-13].

Median OS ranges from 12.6 months (previously treated, avelumab) to 20.3 months (first-line avelumab) in clinical trials [285,287]. 5-year OS rate 26% (prior chemotherapy) and 38% (first-line) with avelumab [285,288].

prognostic_factors: Immunosuppression (solid organ transplant, CLL, HIV, chronic T-cell immunosuppression) is a major risk factor for poor outcomes and recurrence [19,38,40-46,49,114].
Merkel Cell polyomavirus (MCPyV) negative status [w] (associated with higher risk of recurrence [55,57-60]).
Male sex (noted in some studies, but not uniformly confirmed) [w].
Adverse histologic features: infiltrative growth pattern, high tumor depth, lack of brisk TILs, presence of second malignancy (e.g., concurrent SCC) [MS-4, MS-5, 76,80,83,86-91].
prognosis.prognostic_context: MCC prognosis is heavily stage-dependent, with immune status and viral status (MCPyV antibody serostatus) providing additional stratification. Patients who are seronegative for MCPyV oncoprotein antibodies at diagnosis have a significantly higher risk of recurrence and may benefit from more intensive surveillance [55]. Circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) is a promising tool for early detection of recurrence, becoming positive prior to or at time of clinical recurrence [d, 122]. Appropriate staging (including SLNB) and multidisciplinary care are critical for optimizing outcomes.

disease_related.management: SLNB for staging; node dissection and/or RT for positive nodes; systemic therapy for unresectable disease [MCC-2, MCC-4].
Biopsy confirmation; systemic therapy, surgery, RT; consider T-VEC or hyperthermic isolated limb infusion/perfusion [MCC-4].

disease_related.complication: Distant metastases (lung, liver, bone, brain, distant skin/nodes)
disease_related.management: Systemic therapy (immunotherapy preferred), RT for palliation, surgery for oligometastases in highly selected cases; best supportive care [MCC-5].
Treat per recurrence pathway: clinical trial, systemic therapy, RT, surgery; for in-transit recurrence follow N+ in-transit pathway [MCC-6, MCC-4].

disease_related.complication: Second primary malignancies (SCC, BCC, melanoma, CLL, etc.)
disease_related.management: Increased risk; routine skin surveillance and appropriate cancer screening [MS-5, 19,23,35,97-100].

chemotherapy: Common regimens: carboplatin/etoposide, cisplatin/etoposide, CAV (cyclophosphamide/doxorubicin/vincristine), topotecan [MCC-D 3,4].
Reported ORR 40–60% but responses short-lived (median durability 2–9 months) [234,277,306-310].
Toxic death rates 3–10%, higher in older patients [234,277,306].
Adjuvant chemotherapy not routinely recommended; no survival benefit in retrospective studies; chemoradiation may benefit high-risk subsets (positive margins, tumor ≥3 cm) [MCC-D 3, 203,313,314].
immunotherapy: PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitors (avelumab, nivolumab, pembrolizumab, retifanlimab-dlwr) are preferred for advanced disease [MCC-D 2,3,4].
Treatment-related AEs in 68–77%; grade 3/4 AEs in 5–21% [282-287,293,295-297].
Immune-related AEs (colitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies) require clinician and patient education; refer to NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immunotherapy-Related Toxicities [MCC-D 2].
Ipilimumab + nivolumab combination: higher toxicity (grade 3/4 in 40% and 32% in cohorts A/B of Kim et al. phase II) [12,22].
Subcutaneous formulations (nivolumab+hyaluronidase, pembrolizumab+berahyaluronidase) available with different dosing [MCC-D 2,3,4].
targeted_therapy: Pazopanib (tyrosine kinase inhibitor) category 2B for M1 disease; case series show disease stabilization [MCC-D 4, 318,319].
Octreotide LAR (somatostatin analog) if somatostatin receptor positive; disease control in 43% of evaluable patients per retrospective study [MCC-D 4, 317].
surgery: Wide excision with 1–2 cm margins or Mohs/PDEMA; goal of clear margins [MCC-C, MCC-2].
Narrower margins acceptable if adjuvant RT planned [MCC-2, l].
Reconstruction should be delayed until margins verified; primary closure preferred to avoid delay to RT [MCC-C].
SLNB is an important staging tool; impact on OS unclear [MCC-C, a].
Post-surgical complications: wound healing issues, lymphedema risk (especially with groin dissection, high BMI, smoking); caution with RT in lymphedema [MCC-B 5 of 6].
radiation: Adjuvant RT recommended for patients with ≥1 adverse risk factors; delay >7–8 weeks associated with worse outcomes [MCC-B 1, 240].
Conventional fractionation: 50–56 Gy for negative margins, 56–60 Gy for microscopically positive, up to 60–66 Gy for gross positive or high-risk [MCC-B 2,3].
Hypofractionation options: 2.2–2.5 Gy x 20, 3–3.5 Gy x 10, single 8 Gy for low-risk [MCC-B 2,4].
Caution with hypofractionation in immunosuppressed patients due to limited data [MCC-B 3].
Caution with fractions >5 Gy when targets involve cartilage or bone [MCC-B 3].
Caution with hypofractionated RT for draining nodal basin in patients with existing lymphedema or high risk (high BMI, groin, smoker) [MCC-B 5].
Acute effects: dermatitis, fatigue; late effects: fibrosis, lymphedema, secondary malignancy risk (low).
Palliative regimens: 30 Gy/10 fx, 20 Gy/5 fx, 8 Gy x 1–3 fx [MCC-B 2,4].

supportive_care.overview: Best supportive care should be provided to all patients with MCC, following NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care. For patients receiving immune checkpoint inhibitors, management of immune-related adverse events should follow NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immunotherapy-Related Toxicities [MCC-5, MCC-D 2]. The guideline emphasizes minimizing immunosuppression as clinically feasible, as immunosuppression is a risk factor for poor outcomes [MCC-1, e].
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Not explicitly addressed in the MCC guideline; standard nutritional support per institutional practice.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Not explicitly addressed; for chemotherapy regimens (e.g., carboplatin/etoposide), standard antiemetic protocols per NCCN Antiemesis Guidelines are recommended.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Not explicitly addressed; use of G-CSF for febrile neutropenia prophylaxis should follow standard guidelines for chemotherapy regimens.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Not explicitly addressed; VTE prophylaxis per standard recommendations for hospitalized patients with cancer.
supportive_care.pain_management: Not explicitly addressed; pain management per NCCN Adult Cancer Pain Guidelines may be needed for metastatic disease or post-surgical pain.
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Not explicitly addressed; however, regular follow-up visits and patient education on self-examination are recommended. Anxiety and recurrence concerns should be addressed through multidisciplinary care.
supportive_care.dental_care: Not explicitly addressed; general dental care before and during head/neck RT is recommended per standard practice.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: Close clinical follow-up is recommended starting immediately after diagnosis and treatment. Physical examination should include complete skin and complete lymph node examination every 3–6 months for the first 3 years, then every 6–12 months thereafter [MCC-6]. More frequent follow-up is indicated for immunocompromised patients [MCC-6, e]. Imaging and other studies should be performed as clinically indicated (e.g., for emergent adenopathy, organomegaly, new symptoms) [MCC-6]. For high-risk patients (stage IIIB or higher, immunosuppressed), routine imaging surveillance should be considered [MCC-6, v]. MCPyV oncoprotein antibody testing and ctDNA surveillance are often obtained every 3 months [c, d, 55, 122].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: MCC can recur in up to half of patients, with 90% of recurrences occurring within 24 months (median time to recurrence ~8–9 months) [17,18,48,138, MS-21]. Early detection of recurrence may allow more effective salvage therapy. Risk factors for recurrence include immunosuppression, advancing age, stage II–IV, non-SLN metastases, MCPyV-negative status, and other factors as determined by the treating physician [w]. Patients who are seronegative for MCPyV oncoprotein antibodies at diagnosis have higher recurrence risk and may benefit from more intensive surveillance [55]. ctDNA testing becomes positive prior to or at the time of clinically evident recurrence and should be considered for surveillance [122].
late_effects_screening: Second primary malignancies: Patients with MCC have increased risk of developing other skin cancers (SCC, BCC, melanoma), CLL, NHL, lung, breast, kidney cancers, and vice versa [19,23,35,97-100]. Lifelong routine skin surveillance is recommended.
Radiation late effects: fibrosis, lymphedema (especially if groin irradiation), secondary radiation-induced malignancies (rare).
Immunotherapy late effects: chronic endocrinopathies (thyroid, adrenal, pituitary insufficiency) may require long-term hormone replacement.
Surgical late effects: lymphedema after SLNB or node dissection, particularly in groin; scar management.
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Recurrence can be local (at primary site), regional (nodal basin), in-transit (between primary and nodal basin), or disseminated (distant metastases). Time to local recurrence is generally shorter than for regional recurrence; distant metastasis occurs later [16,17,19,225]. Detection of multiple distant metastases at once is not uncommon [125]. For local, locally advanced, or regional recurrence, options include clinical trial, systemic therapy, RT, and/or surgery [MCC-6]. In-transit recurrence follows the Clinical N+ in-transit pathway [MCC-6, MCC-4]. Disseminated recurrence is treated as M1 disease [MCC-6, MCC-5].

clinical_follow_up_schedule.interval: Every 3–6 months for the first 3 years
clinical_follow_up_schedule.components: Physical exam including complete skin and complete lymph node examination [MCC-6]
Physical exam including complete skin and complete lymph node examination [MCC-6]

imaging_strategy: Routine surveillance imaging recommended for high-risk patients (stage ≥IIIB, immunosuppressed) [MCC-6, w].
Modality: diagnostic CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis with oral and IV contrast; neck CT included if primary was on head/neck [MCC-6, v].
Whole-body FDG-PET/CT may be indicated to evaluate for in-transit metastases if primary on extremity [MCC-6, v].
Brain MRI with and without contrast if clinical suspicion of brain metastases or direct extension [MCC-2A, m].
Imaging is also performed as clinically indicated for new symptoms or suspected recurrence [MCC-6].
laboratory_monitoring: MCPyV oncoprotein antibody testing: baseline within 3 months of treatment; surveillance every 3 months in seropositive patients; a rising titer may indicate recurrence [c, 55].
ctDNA assessment: every 3 months for surveillance; can detect recurrence prior to clinical evidence; requires tissue-informed testing [d, 122].
Other laboratory studies as clinically indicated (e.g., liver function tests if concern for metastases) [MCC-1, c].
supportive_follow_up: Patient education on self-examination of the skin and lymph nodes [MCC-6].
Minimization of immunosuppression as clinically feasible in consultation with managing physician [MCC-1, e].
Genetic counseling for patients diagnosed at age <50 years to evaluate for germline mutations in cancer-associated genes [MCC-1, f].
Consider collecting additional tumor tissue from core biopsy for tissue-informed ctDNA testing prior to immunotherapy or RT [MCC-1].
Referral to smoking cessation, nutrition, and physical activity programs as indicated.

special_situations.situation: Immunosuppressed patients (organ transplant, CLL, HIV, chronic immunosuppression)
special_situations.key_message: Immunosuppression is a risk factor for poor outcomes and higher recurrence rates.
special_situations.management: Minimize immunosuppressive treatments as clinically feasible in consultation with the relevant managing physician. More frequent follow-up may be indicated.
