Consider empiric RT to nodal basin if profound immunosuppression [MCC-1, e; MCC-2A, p]. Immunotherapy response may be similar to non-immunosuppressed patients based on limited data [304].
special_situations.evidence: Multiple retrospective studies; NCCN category 2A [MCC-1, e].

special_situations.key_message: Approximately 20% may have germline mutations in cancer-associated genes.
special_situations.management: Genetic counseling should be considered to evaluate for heritable mutations [MCC-1, f].
special_situations.evidence: Mohsin N, et al. JAMA Dermatol 2024;160:172-178 [116].

special_situations.situation: Diagnosis made only with core biopsy
special_situations.key_message: Insufficient tissue may remain for tissue-informed ctDNA testing.
special_situations.management: Consider collecting additional tumor tissue prior to immunotherapy or RT [MCC-1].

special_situations.situation: Locally advanced MCC when curative surgery and RT not feasible
special_situations.key_message: Neoadjuvant nivolumab may be considered in surgical candidates; nonsurgical candidates discuss RT vs systemic therapy.
special_situations.management: Primary treatment: neoadjuvant Nivolumab (if surgical candidate) or RT vs systemic therapy (nonsurgical). Adequate response → proceed to surgical pathway; progression → discuss RT/surgery vs systemic therapy [MCC-3].
special_situations.evidence: CheckMate 358: 47.2% pathologic complete response with neoadjuvant nivolumab [293, 5].

special_situations.key_message: Subcutaneous formulations available for nivolumab and pembrolizumab.
special_situations.management: Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy may substitute for IV nivolumab; pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph may substitute for IV pembrolizumab. Dosing and administration differ [MCC-D 2,3,4].

special_situations.key_message: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab is particularly indicated for disease refractory to PD-1 therapy.
special_situations.management: Consider Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (category 2A) for regional or disseminated disease after progression on anti-PD-1 monotherapy [MCC-D 3,4; 22].
special_situations.evidence: Kim S, et al. Lancet 2022;400:1008-1019: objective response in 31% of patients with prior ICI exposure; grade 3/4 TRAEs 32-40% [22].

special_situations.situation: In-transit disease not amenable to curative surgery/RT
special_situations.key_message: Consider intralesional T-VEC or hyperthermic isolated limb infusion/perfusion (HILP).
special_situations.management: T-VEC for palliation of injectable sites; HILP for limb disease [MCC-4].
special_situations.evidence: Case series and Thiels CA, et al. J Surg Oncol 2016;114:187-192 [t, 315].

clinical_pearls: SLNB is the most reliable staging tool for subclinical nodal disease; it should be performed prior to or at the time of definitive tumor excision, especially in head/neck cases where drainage patterns can be complex [MCC-2, MCC-C, 246].

For patients with ≥1 adverse risk factors (LVI, immunosuppression, tumor >1 cm, positive margins, positive SLNB, head/neck site), adjuvant RT to the primary tumor site should be strongly considered. Delay >7–8 weeks from surgery to RT initiation is associated with worse outcomes [MCC-2A, k; MCC-B 1, 240].
Narrow surgical margins (clear or microscopically positive) are acceptable if adjuvant RT is planned; this approach balances morbidity and avoids delay to RT [MCC-2, l; 179].

Immunotherapy (PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors) is the preferred systemic therapy for advanced MCC over chemotherapy, due to improved durability of response and more favorable safety profile. Chemotherapy should be reserved for patients who are not candidates for immunotherapy or have progressed on it [MCC-D, MS-20].

MCPyV oncoprotein antibody testing and ctDNA monitoring every 3 months can aid in early detection of recurrence. A rising antibody titer in initially seropositive patients or conversion of ctDNA from negative to positive should prompt imaging investigation [c, d, 55, 122].

Multidisciplinary consultation at a center with specialized expertise is recommended for all patients with MCC, particularly those with locally advanced, regional, or disseminated disease, to coordinate optimal sequencing of surgery, RT, and systemic therapy [MCC-1, MCC-3, MCC-4, MCC-5].

For patients with locally advanced MCC who are surgical candidates, neoadjuvant nivolumab can achieve high rates of pathologic complete response (47.2% in CheckMate 358) and may enable R0 resection. Close monitoring for progression is required [MCC-3, 293].

Immunosuppressed patients have worse outcomes and higher recurrence risk; immunosuppression should be minimized when feasible, and patients may require more frequent follow-up. Consider empiric nodal RT if profound immunosuppression compromises SLNB reliability [MCC-1, e; MCC-2A, p].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Merkel Cell Carcinoma, Version 2.2026
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides comprehensive recommendations for diagnosis, staging, treatment, and follow-up of MCC. All recommendations category 2A unless otherwise indicated. Updated October 24, 2025.

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced for best supportive care in MCC patients [MCC-5, MCC-6].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immunotherapy-Related Toxicities
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced for detection and management of immune-related adverse events associated with checkpoint immunotherapies [MCC-D 2].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Squamous Cell Skin Cancer – Principles of PDEMA Technique
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced for description of peripheral and deep en face margin assessment (PDEMA) technique for surgical excision [MCC-2].

guidelines_resources.title: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, 8th Edition (2017)
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides TNM staging classification and prognostic stage groups for MCC; used in the guideline [ST-1, ST-2].

guidelines_resources.title: Protocol for the Examination of Specimens from Patients with Merkel Cell Carcinoma of the Skin, Version 4.0.0.1
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides synoptic reporting guidance for MCC pathology specimens [MS-3, 67].

key_trials.full_name: Avelumab in Patients with Chemotherapy-Refractory Metastatic Merkel Cell Carcinoma
key_trials.population: Patients with stage IV distant MCC progressing after ≥1 prior chemotherapy
key_trials.primary_endpoint: Objective response rate (ORR) by independent review
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Median PFS not reported; durable responses; biomarker analyses [286]
key_trials.practice_change: Established avelumab as effective therapy for chemotherapy-refractory metastatic MCC; led to FDA approval.

key_trials.full_name: First-Line Avelumab in Metastatic Merkel Cell Carcinoma
key_trials.population: First-line treatment for stage IV metastatic MCC
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: PD-L1 positive tumor median OS 38.7 months vs 16.1 months for PD-L1 negative [288]
key_trials.practice_change: First-line immunotherapy for metastatic MCC; increased use of avelumab as standard.

key_trials.full_name: Pembrolizumab for Advanced Merkel Cell Carcinoma (KEYNOTE-017)
key_trials.population: Patients with distant metastatic or locoregional MCC not amenable to definitive surgery/RT, no prior systemic therapy
key_trials.intervention: Pembrolizumab 2 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for up to 2 years
key_trials.key_result: ORR 58% (95% CI, 43.2–71.8); complete response 30%; median PFS 16.8 months (95% CI, 4.6–43.4); 3-year OS 59.4% (89.5% for responders) [296,297]
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Median OS not reached; durable responses; safety profile consistent with pembrolizumab
key_trials.practice_change: Pembrolizumab is a preferred first-line therapy for advanced MCC; supported FDA approval.

key_trials.full_name: Neoadjuvant Nivolumab for Resectable Merkel Cell Carcinoma
key_trials.population: Resectable MCC stage IIA–IV (M1 with resectable disease) with ≥1 dose of neoadjuvant nivolumab
key_trials.intervention: Neoadjuvant nivolumab 240 mg IV every 2 weeks for up to 4 doses before surgery
key_trials.key_result: pCR in 47.2% (17/36) of patients who underwent surgery; radiographic tumor reduction ≥30% in 54.5% of evaluable patients [293]
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Median recurrence-free survival and overall survival not reached at median follow-up 20.3 months
key_trials.practice_change: Neoadjuvant nivolumab is included as a useful option in certain circumstances for locally advanced MCC where curative surgery and RT are not feasible [MCC-3].

key_trials.full_name: Retifanlimab in Patients with Advanced or Metastatic Merkel Cell Carcinoma
key_trials.population: Unresectable locally advanced or metastatic MCC, no prior anti-PD-1/L1 therapy
key_trials.key_result: ORR 46.2% (30/65); complete response 12.3%; disease control rate 53.8% [298]
key_trials.practice_change: Retifanlimab-dlwr is listed as a preferred regimen for many disease states in the guideline, indicating its emerging role.

key_trials.full_name: Combined Nivolumab and Ipilimumab with or without Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy for Advanced Merkel Cell Carcinoma: A Randomised, Open Label, Phase 2 Trial
key_trials.population: Advanced MCC, including both ICI-naïve and prior ICI-exposed patients
key_trials.intervention: Nivolumab + ipilimumab ± SBRT (cohort A with SBRT, cohort B without)
key_trials.comparator: Two cohorts (non-randomized comparison of SBRT addition)
key_trials.key_result: ORR 100% (22/22) in ICI-naïve (9 complete responses, 41%); ORR 31% (8/26) in prior ICI; no significant difference in ORR with SBRT (P=0.26) [22]
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Grade 3/4 TRAEs: 40% cohort A, 32% cohort B; median follow-up 14.6 months
key_trials.practice_change: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab is recommended as other recommended regimen for regional/disseminated disease and particularly indicated for PD-1 refractory disease [MCC-D 3,4].
